7. Transformers (Sara Beery)

7.1. View as GNNs and Token Networks

  • A GNN works as follows: at layer , each neigbor sends a message that depends on its embeding and possibly edge features. The node pools all incommming messages with an aggregation function like mean or max. The node then updates its embedding based on its current embedding and the aggregated message. Each layer expands the receptive field by one.

    • For example, the message function might be a linear transformation, the aggregate function can be a sum, and the update function can be an MLP on the message plus the residual.
    • Expressivity limit: GNNs provably cannot distinguish certain non-isomorphic graphs.
    • Since then GNNs have been superseded by graph transformers, which include a bias term in the softmax to encode edge/graph features.

A transformer can be viewed as a GNN over a fully-connected graph, or more generally a token network with self-attention and MLP as the aggregation and nonlinear steps:

  • Multihead self attention: have multiple heads and aggregate the results using a weight matrix.

What if we want to train a transformer to take an image and a question and output something? One simple strategy is to concatenate the image and text tokens, and let self-attention figure out the interactions (e.g. LXMERT, UNITER). We can also alternate between cross attention and self-attention. For example, text attending to image: the queries come from the text and the keys and values come from the image and the text gets updated to incorporate image information. More explicitly, let our text sequence be and our image sequence be . Then for text attending to image we have

(16)

(17)

The final answer can be read from a text [CLS] token or pooled across all text and/or image tokens. In attention pooling, we use a learnable query vector , and the output is a weighted combination of the tokens, determined by keys and values. The model learns to route useful information to the CLS token, and in attention pooling the model learns what parts of the suqence to include. The two use the same mechanism under the hood.

Dumb remark: a transformer is kind of like a CNN.

7.2. Positional Encoding

We can enforce causality using masked attention.

We encode position by concatenating a representation of the position vectors. We dont represent them as scalars (would grow without bound, see https://piazza.com/class/mexb078f4z54ia/post/174) but rather the vector of values a set of sine and cosine waves take on at each position:

Standard (additive) approach: Define a learned embedding that maps index/location to a vector and add that position vector on top of your original embedding. For the additition case we don’t just use the raw coordinates as because that does not live in the same dimension/latent space.

We are assuming that and have comparable magnitudes. We choose the token embeddings to have roughly the same magnitude.

7.3. Autoregressive (e.g. GPT)

Decoder:

  • Take all words in context window
  • Use causal mask

  • Take output embedding on last hidden state and pass it through classification head to get next token.

    • We can use nucleus sampling (selecting randomly from the subset of top tokens whose cumulative threshold exceeds ) to increase diversity, as opposed to greedy decoding.

We train with a causal mask since during generation we can’t see into the future.

Original transformer paper used encoder-decoder, where the decoder sequence cross attends to the image plus causal mask while generating new stuff. That is used for sequence to sequence tasks like translation or image-to-text. Decoder starts with <bos> (beginning of sentence token).
